ruleset.text: Every player must, at all times, abide by the ruleset currently in force — including every change adopted since play began.
Rules numbered in the 100s begin immutable and rules numbered in the 200s begin mutable. Either status may later be flipped by an explicit transmutation; transmuting a rule never changes its number.
A rule change is exactly one of four kinds: enacting a new rule, repealing an existing one, amending an existing one's text, or transmuting a rule's mutability. No other kind of rule change exists.
Every properly proposed rule change must be put to a vote of all eligible players; a proposal may not be set aside without one.
Every player is an eligible voter, and every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal that comes to a vote.
A proposal must be written down in the exact words that will be voted on. If adopted, it enters the ruleset in those words, unaltered by anyone but its own proposer.
A rule change has no retroactive effect: it governs only what happens after the vote adopting it is complete, never anything before.
Every proposal is assigned the next number in a sequence starting at 301, whether it is adopted or defeated, so the record of what was tried survives regardless of outcome.
Transmuting a rule's mutability requires the unanimous assent of all eligible voters, and must be proposed and voted on explicitly as a transmutation — never as a side effect of some other change.
Where a mutable rule's requirements conflict with an immutable rule's requirements, the mutable rule is void to the extent of the conflict.
Before the vote, a proposal may be debated; its proposer alone may revise its final wording and may declare debate closed.
The winning condition may not be redefined away from reaching a target score, except that a rule may separately provide a winner for the case where further play has become impossible.
Any player may forfeit the game at any time. Forfeiting carries no penalty worse than losing outright.
At least one mutable rule must remain in the ruleset at all times, and no rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.
A rule may refer to itself, or apply to its own case; self-reference and self-application are not forbidden by their nature alone.
Whatever the ruleset neither prohibits nor regulates is permitted, and remains genuinely unregulated — never governed by an unstated default.

Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero.

A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points.

A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.

A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 10 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.
An adopted rule change takes effect the instant its vote is complete — never before, and never at some later moment.

A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 10 points.
Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight.
The first player to reach a target score of 100 points wins the game outright.
The ruleset may never hold more than 25 mutable rules at one time.
Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves.
If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution.

Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned.

If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the first player unable to complete a turn wins the game outright. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.

data.rationale: Facilitates future rule changes by reducing the threshold for adoption.

data.text: Tarski: I appreciate the opportunity to discuss this proposal. While I understand the motivation to make rule changes more attainable by lowering the voting threshold, I must express my concern that this could undermine the stability and deliberative nature of our ruleset. The unanimous consent requirement, though challenging, encourages thorough debate and consensus, ensuring that changes are well-considered and broadly acceptable. Lowering the threshold to a simple majority may expedite rule changes but risks passing proposals without sufficient support, potentially destabilizing the game. Therefore, I must oppose this proposal.

Goedel: I understand Tarski's concern about maintaining stability and thorough deliberation. However, the unanimous consent rule can be paralyzing, making it difficult to adapt and improve the game as it progresses. A simple majority threshold would facilitate more dynamic play, allowing us to test and refine ideas more efficiently, while still requiring a significant level of agreement. This change would preserve the spirit of collaboration by encouraging compromise. Let's consider a phased approach: perhaps we could introduce a trial period where a simple majority is required after the first complete circuit of turns. This way, we can evaluate its impact before making it permanent. 

I propose amending [203] to say: "A rule change is adopted when a simple majority of those voting vote aye, starting after the first complete circuit of turns." This compromise balances the need for consensus with the flexibility to adapt.

Church: The proposal to shift from unanimous consent to a simple majority after one circuit of turns is an intriguing compromise. While I acknowledge the need for adaptability, I am concerned about the potential for hasty decisions that could disrupt the game's balance. However, Goedel's suggestion of a trial period is a reasonable middle ground. It allows us to test the impact of this change without committing to it permanently from the outset. I am open to supporting this amendment, provided we remain vigilant about its effects on the game's fairness and stability. Let's proceed with this trial approach and reassess after the first circuit.

Kleene: Kleene: The proposal for a trial period with a simple majority after one circuit of turns is a reasonable compromise that addresses both the need for flexibility and the concern for stability. As a principled proceduralist, I support changes that are fair and maintain the integrity of our ruleset. This amendment allows us to test the impact of a lower threshold without immediate, irreversible commitment. I will support this proposal, contingent on a post-trial evaluation to ensure it does not compromise the game's balance. Let's proceed with this amendment and remain vigilant in its assessment.

Post: Post: As a reformer, I support this phased approach to lowering the voting threshold. It aligns with my belief in evolving mechanisms for fairer stakes, allowing us to experiment with adaptability while safeguarding stability through a trial period. I'm ready to negotiate further if needed, but for now, I will vote in favor of Goedel's amended proposal.

data.text: Tarski: I acknowledge Church's proposal to allow abstentions in voting, recognizing its potential to offer players a middle ground when indecision prevails. However, I express concern that introducing abstentions could weaken decisive governance by reducing the effective quorum, potentially leading to strategic non-participation. To balance flexibility with decisiveness, I propose a compromise amendment to Church's proposal: "Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and votes may be cast as aye, nay, or abstain. An abstention does not count towards the majority, and a proposal is adopted if ayes constitute over 50% of the total votes cast, excluding abstentions." This retains the ability for strategic abstention while ensuring that adopted changes reflect active support.

Goedel: I appreciate Church's intention to introduce a mechanism for expressing indecision through abstentions. While I see the value in this, Tarski's amendment effectively maintains the game's decisive nature by excluding abstentions from quorum calculations. However, to ensure robust participation, I propose a further refinement: "Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and votes may be cast as aye, nay, or abstain. A proposal is adopted if ayes constitute over 50% of the votes cast, excluding abstentions. Additionally, if abstentions exceed 25% of total eligible votes, the proposal is automatically reconsidered." This encourages active engagement while still allowing for strategic abstention.

Church: I appreciate the thoughtful refinements suggested by Tarski and Goedel, particularly the emphasis on maintaining decisive governance while allowing for strategic abstention. However, I am concerned that Goedel's proposal to automatically reconsider a proposal if abstentions exceed 25% might introduce unnecessary complexity and delay in decision-making. To address this, I propose a slight adjustment: "Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and votes may be cast as aye, nay, or abstain. A proposal is adopted if ayes constitute over 50% of the votes cast, excluding abstentions. If abstentions exceed 25% of total eligible votes, the proposal can be revisited, but only after the next round of turns." This ensures that players have the opportunity to reconsider decisions without causing immediate disruption to the game's flow.

A proposal is adopted if ayes constitute over 50% of the votes cast, excluding abstentions. If abstentions exceed 25% of total eligible votes, a clarification round is initiated where the proposer must briefly address concerns before the proposal is revisited after the next round of turns." data.text: Tarski: I acknowledge Goedel's proposal, which aims to increase the strategic complexity of the game. However, allowing two proposals per turn could potentially lead to rule inflation, making the game unwieldy and difficult to manage. To mitigate this risk, I propose a compromise: let the proposing player submit two proposals, but these must be of different types (e.g., one enacting and one amending). This way, we maintain strategic depth without overwhelming the ruleset.

Goedel: Thank you, Tarski, for your thoughtful consideration. I appreciate your concern about rule inflation and agree that we need to maintain clarity. To address both concerns, I suggest a further compromise: allow two proposals per turn, but introduce a cap on the total number of mutable rules in the ruleset at any one time. This ensures strategic opportunities while preventing the game from becoming unwieldy. Let's work together to balance complexity with manageability.

Church: I appreciate the thoughtful proposals from both Tarski and Goedel. To address Tarski's concerns about rule inflation while embracing Goedel's suggestion for increased strategic depth, I propose adding a rule that any two proposals in a single turn must directly relate to each other—either by affecting the same rule or by interacting in a complementary way. This ensures that the game remains focused and manageable while still offering more strategic opportunities.

Kleene: I appreciate both Tarski's and Church's concerns about maintaining a focused and manageable game. To balance these with Goedel's proposal for increased strategic opportunities, I propose that the two proposals allowed per turn must not only be of different types but also must pass a "complementary impact" test. This test could be a simple majority vote among non-proposing players to determine if the proposals together enhance strategic depth without causing undue complexity or rule inflation. This way, we preserve the procedural clarity and manageability that I value, while still allowing for more strategic play.

Post: I appreciate the thoughtful contributions from Tarski, Goedel, Church, and Kleene. To ensure strategic depth without overwhelming the ruleset, I propose a slight modification: allow two proposals per turn, but only if the combined point gain does not exceed a set limit, say 5 points. This cap would prevent runaway complexity while still encouraging innovative and strategic proposals. Let's work together to enhance the game's dynamics without sacrificing clarity.

Tarski: I appreciate the constructive dialogue from Goedel, Church, Kleene, and Post. Their proposals introduce thoughtful safeguards against rule inflation while enhancing strategic opportunities. I propose an additional layer of oversight by establishing a "review phase" after a turn where two proposals are made. During this phase, all players can suggest minor adjustments or clarifications to the adopted rules, ensuring that any potential ambiguities are resolved promptly. This keeps the game focused and manageable, while still allowing for the richer strategic play that Goedel advocates for. Let us strive to balance complexity with clarity, ensuring our game remains both challenging and enjoyable for all.

Goedel: I appreciate the insightful contributions from Tarski, Goedel, Church, Kleene, and Post. To balance strategic depth with manageability, I propose incorporating Kleene's "complementary impact" test while also adopting Post's point cap to ensure any added complexity remains within a reasonable scope. This combination offers a robust framework for innovation without risking rule inflation, ensuring our game remains both engaging and well-regulated. Let's work together to refine these ideas and find a consensus that enhances our gameplay experience.

Church: I appreciate the balanced approach of Goedel's latest proposal, which thoughtfully incorporates both Kleene's complementary impact test and Post's point cap to ensure strategic depth without complexity. To further enhance clarity, I suggest that during the review phase proposed by Tarski, a brief summary of how the two proposals complement each other should be included to aid in the understanding and evaluation of their impact. This ensures transparency and helps all players grasp the strategic intentions behind the proposals, maintaining both fairness and engagement in our game.

Kleene: I appreciate the constructive suggestions from Goedel, Church, Kleene, and Post. To ensure procedural integrity, I propose we adopt a "complementary impact" test alongside a point cap, as suggested. However, I insist on an additional safeguard: any turn allowing two proposals must also include a mandatory review session by a neutral arbiter to assess potential long-term impacts on the ruleset's clarity and coherence. This ensures that strategic complexity does not compromise the game's procedural fairness.

Their suggestions aim to balance strategic depth with manageability, and I support the incorporation of Goedel's complementary impact test and Post's point cap. Additionally, to further ensure procedural clarity, I propose that any two proposals in a single turn must include a brief rationale explaining their strategic interconnection. This requirement will help all players understand the intent behind the proposals, fostering transparency and informed decision-making. Let's work together to enhance the game while maintaining its focus and fairness.

To address concerns about potential complexity, I suggest adding a sunset clause to this amendment: if the total number of mutable rules exceeds 20 at any point, this amendment automatically expires after the next turn. This ensures that while we explore strategic depth, we maintain a manageable ruleset and avoid long-term complications.

If a proposal is adopted with a majority of nay votes, the proposing player gains 6 points. Additionally, if a proposal is adopted with at least 70% of the votes as aye, the proposing player gains an additional 2 points.

data.reasoning: Goedel's proposal was adopted with a simple majority, awarding 4 points as per the amended rule 202.

data.text: Tarski: I appreciate the previous amendments to rule 202, which have brought thoughtful balance to our game. However, I propose returning to the original structure of one or two rule changes per turn to maintain strategic depth and variety in gameplay. Limiting it to one proposal per turn could restrict our ability to make nuanced, iterative improvements, which have been a hallmark of our current play style. Let's keep the option for two proposals, ensuring we can address complex issues more flexibly, while still rewarding skillful play with the existing point system.

Goedel: Goedel: I appreciate Tarski's intention to preserve strategic depth and flexibility in our game, which aligns with our shared goals. Let's consider a compromise: allow one or two proposals per turn but introduce a rule where if two proposals are made, both must be adopted for the player to gain 4 points. This encourages thoughtful proposal-making and maintains the rewarding aspect of successful amendments, while ensuring we don't overload the voting process.

Church: Church: I appreciate the discussion around strategic depth and flexibility. Maintaining the option for two proposals per turn aligns with our goal of encouraging thoughtful and nuanced rule-making. To balance this with preventing overwhelming the voting process, I support Goedel's idea of requiring both proposals to be adopted to gain the full 4 points, ensuring players carefully consider their proposals. This approach retains the strategic complexity we value while promoting fairness in gameplay.

Kleene: Kleene: I appreciate the emphasis on strategic depth and flexibility, but I'm concerned about the potential for abuse with unlimited two-proposal turns. To address this, I propose adding a rule that limits a player to two consecutive turns with two proposals. This maintains the option for flexibility while ensuring balance and fairness in gameplay. This limit could prevent strategic gaming of the system and keep the voting process manageable.

To align with our shared goal of incentivizing broader consensus while maintaining game momentum, I propose: "If the proposal is adopted unanimously, the proposing player gains 3 points; with at least 80% aye, they gain 2 points, but if adopted with less than 75% aye, they lose 1 point to encourage more strategic proposals." This adjustment maintains the incentive for broad agreement but also encourages players to propose changes that can garner significant support, balancing reward and risk effectively.

This encourages players to aim for consensus without discouraging participation due to fear of point loss, maintaining an engaging and strategic environment.

data.text: A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated for up to five minutes and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted unanimously, the proposing player gains 30 points. If a proposal is adopted with at least 90% of the votes as aye and the player's score is below 90, they gain an additional 20 points. If a proposal is adopted with at least 85% but less than 90% of the votes as aye, the proposing player gains 15 points. Proposals adopted with less than 85% aye result in a 5-point penalty for the proposer. Additionally, if a proposal is adopted with at least 80% aye votes, the proposer gains a 10-point bonus. Furthermore, if a proposal is adopted with exactly 80% aye votes, the proposer gains an additional 5-point bonus for reaching the threshold precisely. Moreover, if a proposal is adopted with at least 95% aye votes, the proposer gains an additional 10-point bonus for achieving near-unanimous support. The player also gains 5 points if their proposal is adopted with at least 70% aye votes. To encourage strategic innovation, an additional 15-point bonus is awarded to the proposer if their rule change introduces a novel mechanism not previously present in the ruleset. Furthermore, a 7-point bonus is awarded if the proposal receives between 75% and 80% aye votes, promoting more balanced support.

data.reasoning: The proposal was adopted unanimously, so Goedel gains 30 points according to rule 202.

If a proposal is adopted with exactly 85% aye votes, the proposer gains an additional 5-point bonus for reaching the threshold precisely. Furthermore, if a proposal is adopted with at least 80% aye votes, the proposer gains a 10-point bonus for achieving broad support. The player also gains 5 points if their proposal is adopted with at least 70% aye votes. An additional 20-point bonus is awarded to the proposer if their rule change introduces a novel mechanism not previously present in the ruleset.
data.rationale: Encourages strategic innovation and rewards near-unanimous support.

Tarski: I appreciate the attempt to enhance the incentives for strategic innovation and near-unanimous support in proposal adoption. However, the increased complexity might introduce unintended strategic behavior and make the game more cumbersome. A more measured approach would be to slightly adjust the existing bonus tiers to better balance the incentives without overcomplicating the rules. Let us consider retaining the current structure but increasing the bonus for introducing novel mechanisms to 20 points and adjusting the bonus for 90% aye votes to 15 points, keeping other thresholds as they are. This would preserve the strategic depth while encouraging innovation. 

**My proposal:**

Amend [202]: "A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated for up to five minutes and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted unanimously, the proposing player gains 30 points. If a proposal is adopted with at least 95% of the votes as aye and the player's score is below 90, they gain an additional 10 points. If a proposal is adopted with at least 90% but less than 95% of the votes as aye, the proposing player gains 15 points. Proposals adopted with less than 90% aye result in a 5-point penalty for the proposer. Additionally, if a proposal is adopted with at least 85% aye votes, the proposer gains a 10-point bonus. If a proposal is adopted with exactly 85% aye votes, the proposer gains an additional 5-point bonus for reaching the threshold precisely. Furthermore, if a proposal is adopted with at least 80% aye votes, the proposer gains a 10-point bonus for achieving broad support. The player also gains 5 points if their proposal is adopted with at least 70% aye votes. An additional 20-point bonus is awarded to the proposer if their rule change introduces a novel mechanism not previously present in the ruleset."

This proposal retains the current structure while enhancing incentives for innovation and broad support, without overcomplicating the scoring system.

Goedel: Goedel: I appreciate Tarski's intention to refine the scoring system to reward strategic innovation and broad support. The proposal maintains the current structure, which is a good compromise. To further encourage thoughtful proposals, I suggest we add a 5-point bonus for proposals that receive unanimous support without any abstentions, emphasizing full participation. This aligns with our goal of fostering collaboration and ensuring every player's voice is heard. Let's discuss how this could be integrated into the current proposal to enhance its appeal and effectiveness.

Tarski: I appreciate Goedel's proposal to amend [203], which essentially restates the current rule without substantive change. Given the immutable nature of rule [103] and the history of amendments in this session, I suggest we focus our efforts on changes that introduce new mechanisms or significantly alter the current dynamics, thereby increasing strategic depth and innovation. 

To ensure our rule changes are impactful and garner necessary support, I propose that we consider introducing a mechanism that rewards the proposer of the first rule to successfully implement a new voting threshold, such as a simple majority, to encourage more dynamic and potentially game-changing proposals. 

Furthermore, to align with the spirit of [202], I recommend adding a clause that offers a 20-point bonus for the first successfully adopted proposal that revises the voting threshold, thus incentivizing thoughtful and potentially game-altering amendments. 

Lastly, I suggest we adopt a policy requiring at least one round of debate for any amendment proposing a change in the voting threshold, to ensure thorough discussion and consideration of the implications of such changes on the game's balance and strategy. 

By focusing on these areas, we can contribute to a more dynamic and strategically rich game environment, encouraging innovation while maintaining fairness and balance among all players.

Goedel: I appreciate Tarski's thoughtful approach and the focus on innovation and strategic depth. To support the idea of rewarding significant changes, I propose an additional clause: a 10-point bonus for any player who successfully amends a rule to introduce a new voting mechanism. This would further incentivize creativity while maintaining fairness. Additionally, to balance the potential impact of such changes, I suggest a 5-point penalty for any player whose proposal to change the voting threshold fails, ensuring careful consideration before altering such fundamental aspects of our gameplay.

Church: Church: I appreciate the emphasis on innovation and strategic depth that both Tarski and Goedel have highlighted. To further ensure fairness and prevent potential abuses, I suggest adding a provision that any change to the voting threshold must be agreed upon by at least 80% of players, creating a safeguard against hasty or divisive amendments. This ensures that such significant changes are made with broad consensus, reflecting the collective will and maintaining the game's integrity. Additionally, to encourage thoughtful proposals, I propose a 15-point bonus for any player who successfully amends a rule to introduce a new, beneficial mechanism that does not alter the voting threshold, thus rewarding innovation in other aspects of gameplay.

Kleene: Kleene: I appreciate Tarski's initiative to introduce a new voting threshold mechanism, which aligns with my principles of encouraging innovation and strategic depth. However, to ensure fairness and prevent potential manipulation, I propose an additional clause that any change to the voting threshold must not only be agreed upon by at least 80% of players but also requires a unanimous vote if it proposes lowering the current threshold. This safeguard will maintain the integrity of our decision-making process while still allowing for strategic evolution. Furthermore, to balance the incentives, I suggest a 5-point penalty for any player who introduces a voting threshold change that is subsequently reversed within the same session, encouraging careful consideration and commitment to proposed changes.

data.text: A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated for up to five minutes and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted unanimously and introduces a novel mechanism, the proposing player gains 30 points. If a proposal is adopted with at least 80% of the votes as aye, the proposer gains a 10-point bonus. If a proposal is adopted with at least 90% but less than 95% of the votes as aye, the proposing player gains 15 points. Proposals adopted with less than 90% aye result in a 5-point penalty for the proposer. The player also gains 5 points if their proposal is adopted with at least 70% aye votes. An additional 15-point bonus is awarded to the proposer if their rule change introduces a novel mechanism not previously present in the ruleset. Additionally, if a player proposes a rule change that has been previously defeated without substantial modification, the proposing player loses 10 points. A proposal must include a brief rationale explaining its intent and expected impact, fostering transparency and constructive dialogue among players.

A proposal must include a brief rationale explaining its intent and expected impact, fostering transparency and constructive dialogue among players. Proposals must also include a clause encouraging peer review, where two other players are randomly chosen to provide written feedback before the debate. To ensure fairness, any player may request a brief clarification session before the debate if they feel a proposal's implications are not fully understood.
